From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Representation of the Ukrainian language using Latin letters

For the romanization of Ukrainian on the English Wikipedia, see [Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Romanization of Ukrainian](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Romanization_of_Ukrainian "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Romanization of Ukrainian")

The **romanization of Ukrainian** is the representation of the [Ukrainian language](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language") in the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet"). Ukrainian is written in [its own alphabet](/wiki/Ukrainian_alphabet "Ukrainian alphabet"), which is based on the [Cyrillic script](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script"). [Romanization](/wiki/Romanization "Romanization") may be employed to represent Ukrainian text or [pronunciation](/wiki/Pronunciation "Pronunciation") for non-Ukrainian readers, on computer systems that cannot reproduce Cyrillic characters, or for typists who are not familiar with the Ukrainian [keyboard layout](/wiki/Keyboard_layout "Keyboard layout"). Methods of romanization include [transliteration](/wiki/Transliteration "Transliteration") (representing written text) and [transcription](/wiki/Transcription_(linguistics) "Transcription (linguistics)") (representing the spoken word).

In contrast to romanization, there have been several historical proposals for a [Ukrainian Latin alphabet](/wiki/Ukrainian_Latin_alphabet "Ukrainian Latin alphabet"), usually based on those used by [West Slavic languages](/wiki/West_Slavic_languages "West Slavic languages"), but none have been widely accepted.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Romanization systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Ukrainian&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Romanization systems")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Uzhevych_Arras_MS-6b.png/250px-Uzhevych_Arras_MS-6b.png)](/wiki/File:Uzhevych_Arras_MS-6b.png)

Part of a table of letters of the alphabet for the [Ruthenian language](/wiki/Ruthenian_language "Ruthenian language"), from [Ivan Uzhevych](/wiki/Ivan_Uzhevych "Ivan Uzhevych")'s *Hrammatyka Slovenskaja* (1645). Columns show the letter names printed, in manuscript Cyrillic and Latin, common Cyrillic letterforms, and the Latin transliteration. ([Part 2](/wiki/File:Uzhevych_Arras_MS-7a.png "File:Uzhevych Arras MS-7a.png") and [part 3](/wiki/File:Uzhevych_Arras_MS-7b.png "File:Uzhevych Arras MS-7b.png").)

### Transliteration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Ukrainian&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Transliteration")]

[Transliteration](/wiki/Transliteration "Transliteration") is the letter-for-letter representation of text using another [writing system](/wiki/Writing_system "Writing system"). [Rudnyckyj](/wiki/Jaroslav_Rudnyckyj "Jaroslav Rudnyckyj") classified transliteration systems into scientific transliteration, used in academic and especially linguistic works, and practical systems, used in administration, journalism, in the postal system, in schools, etc.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Scientific transliteration, also called the scholarly system, is used internationally, with very little variation, while the various practical methods of transliteration are adapted to the orthographical conventions of other languages, like English, French, German, etc.

Depending on the purpose of the transliteration it may be necessary to be able to reconstruct the original text, or it may be preferable to have a transliteration which sounds like the original language when read aloud.

#### Scientific transliteration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Ukrainian&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Scientific transliteration")]

*[Scientific transliteration](/wiki/Scientific_transliteration_of_Cyrillic "Scientific transliteration of Cyrillic")*, also called the *academic*, *linguistic*, *international*, or *scholarly* system, is most often seen in linguistic publications on Slavic languages. It is purely phonemic, meaning each character represents one meaningful unit of sound, and is based on the [Croatian Latin alphabet](/wiki/Croatian_Latin_alphabet "Croatian Latin alphabet").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Different variations are appropriate to represent the phonology of historical Old Ukrainian (mid 11th–14th centuries) and Middle Ukrainian (15th–18th centuries).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

A variation was codified in the 1898 Prussian Instructions for libraries, or *Preußische Instruktionen* (PI), and widely used in bibliographic cataloguing in Central Europe and Scandinavia. With further modifications it was published by the International Organization for Standardization as recommendation [ISO/R 9](/wiki/ISO/R_9 "ISO/R 9") in 1954, revised in 1968, and again as an international standard in 1986 and 1995.

Representing all of the necessary diacritics on computers requires [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode"), [Latin-2](/wiki/Latin-2 "Latin-2"), [Latin-4](/wiki/Latin-4 "Latin-4"), or [Latin-7](/wiki/Latin-7 "Latin-7") encoding. Other Slavic based romanizations occasionally seen are those based on the [Slovak alphabet](/wiki/Slovak_alphabet "Slovak alphabet") or the [Polish alphabet](/wiki/Polish_alphabet "Polish alphabet"), which include symbols for palatalized consonants.

#### Library of Congress system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Ukrainian&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Library of Congress system")]

The *ALA-LC Romanization Tables* were first discussed by the American Library Association in 1885,[[4]](#cite_note-4) and published in 1904 and 1908,[[5]](#cite_note-5) including rules for romanizing Church Slavic, the pre-reform Russian alphabet, and Serbo-Croatian.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Revised tables including Ukrainian were published in 1941,[[7]](#cite_note-7) and remain in use virtually unchanged according to the latest 2011 release.[[8]](#cite_note-8) This system is used to represent bibliographic information by US and Canadian libraries, by the British Library since 1975,[[9]](#cite_note-British_Library-9) and in North American publications.

In addition to bibliographic cataloguing, simplified versions of the Library of Congress system are widely used for romanization in the text of academic and general publications. For notes or bibliographical references, some publications use a version without ligatures, which offers sufficient precision but simplifies the typesetting burden and easing readability. For specialist audiences or those familiar with Slavic languages, a version without ligatures and diacritical marks is sometimes used.[[10]](#cite_note-:2-10) For broader audiences, a "modified Library of Congress system" is employed for personal, organizational, and place names, omitting all ligatures and diacritics, ignoring the soft sign ь (ʹ), with initial Є- (*I͡E-*), Й- (*Ĭ-*), Ю- ( *I͡U-*), and Я- (*I͡A-*) represented by *Ye-*, *Y-*, *Yu-*, and *Ya-*, surnames' terminal -ий (*-yĭ*) and -ій (*-iĭ*) endings simplified to *-y*, and sometimes with common first names anglicized, for example, Олександр (*Oleksandr*) written as *Alexander*.

:   | Typical use | Variation | Example |
    | --- | --- | --- |
    | Original Cyrillic text | – | [Ярослав Рудницький](/wiki/Jaroslav_Rudnyckyj "Jaroslav Rudnyckyj") |
    | Library catalogue, standalone bibliography | Strict ALA-LC | I͡Aroslav Rudnyt͡sʹkyĭ |
    | Footnote or bibliography | Without ligatures | Iaroslav Rudnytsʹkyĭ |
    | Academic text | Without ligatures or diacritics | Iaroslav Rudnytskyi |
    | Names in general text | Modified Library of Congress | Yaroslav Rudnytsky |
    | Ukrainian National transliteration | Modified [BGN/PCGN](/wiki/BGN/PCGN_romanization "BGN/PCGN romanization") | Yaroslav Rudnytskyi |

Similar principles were systematically described for Russian by J. Thomas Shaw in 1969,[[11]](#cite_note-11) and since widely adopted. Their application for Ukrainian and multilingual text were described in the 1984 English translation of Kubiiovych's *[Encyclopedia of Ukraine](/wiki/Encyclopedia_of_Ukraine "Encyclopedia of Ukraine")*[[12]](#cite_note-12) and in the 1997 translation of Hrushevskyi's *[History of Ukraine-Rusʹ](/wiki/History_of_Ukraine-Rus%CA%B9 "History of Ukraine-Rusʹ")*,[[13]](#cite_note-13) and other sources have referred to these, for example, historian [Serhii Plokhy](/wiki/Serhii_Plokhy "Serhii Plokhy") in several works. However, the details of usage vary, for example, the authors of the *Historical Dictionary of Ukraine* render the soft sign ь before о with an *i*, "thus Khvyliovy, not Khvylovy, as in the *Encyclopedia of Ukraine*".[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Requires Unicode for connecting diacritics, but only plain ASCII characters for a simplified version.

#### British Standard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Ukrainian&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: British Standard")]

*British Standard 2979:1958 "Transliteration of Cyrillic and Greek Characters"*,[[15]](#cite_note-15) from [BSI](/wiki/BSI_Group "BSI Group"), is used by the Oxford University Press.[[16]](#cite_note-Oxford_Style_Manual-16) A variation is used by the British Museum and British Library, but since 1975 their new acquisitions have been catalogued using Library of Congress transliteration.[[9]](#cite_note-British_Library-9)

In addition to the "British" system, the standard also includes tables for the "International" system for Cyrillic, corresponding to ISO/R 9:1968 (and ISO's recommendation reciprocally has an alternate system corresponding to BSI's).[[17]](#cite_note-:0-17) It also includes tables for romanization of Greek.

#### BGN/PCGN

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Ukrainian&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: BGN/PCGN")]

*[BGN/PCGN romanization](/wiki/BGN/PCGN_romanization "BGN/PCGN romanization")* is a series of standards approved by the [United States Board on Geographic Names](/wiki/United_States_Board_on_Geographic_Names "United States Board on Geographic Names") and Permanent Committee on Geographical Names for British Official Use. Pronunciation is intuitive for English-speakers. For Ukrainian, the former BGN/PCGN system was adopted in 1965, but superseded there by the [Ukrainian National System](#Ukrainian_National_transliteration) in 2019.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) A modified version is also mentioned in the Oxford Style Manual.[[16]](#cite_note-Oxford_Style_Manual-16)

Requires only ASCII characters if optional separators are not used.

#### GOST (1971, 1983)/Derzhstandart (1995, 2021)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Ukrainian&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: GOST (1971, 1983)/Derzhstandart (1995, 2021)")]

The Soviet Union's [GOST](/wiki/GOST "GOST"), [COMECON](/wiki/COMECON "COMECON")'s SEV, and Ukraine's [Derzhstandart](/wiki/Derzhspozhivstandard "Derzhspozhivstandard") are government standards bodies of the former Eurasian communist countries. They published a series of romanization systems for Ukrainian,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] which were replaced by ISO 9:1995. For details, see [GOST 16876-71](/wiki/GOST_16876-71 "GOST 16876-71").

#### DSTU 9112:2021

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Ukrainian&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: DSTU 9112:2021")]

Main article: [DSTU 9112:2021](/wiki/DSTU_9112:2021 "DSTU 9112:2021")

On 1 April 2022, the ["Cyrillic-Latin transliteration and Latin-Cyrillic retransliteration of Ukrainian texts. Writing rules" (ДСТУ 9112:2021)](/wiki/DSTU_9112:2021 "DSTU 9112:2021") was approved as [State Standard of Ukraine](https://uk.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%94%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B6%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%BD%D1%96_%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B4%D0%B0%D1%80%D1%82%D0%B8_%D0%A3%D0%BA%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%97%D0%BD%D0%B8 "uk:Державні стандарти України"). The standard is based on modified [ISO 9:1995](/wiki/ISO_9:1995 "ISO 9:1995") standard and was developed by the Technical Committee 144 "Information and Documentation" of the [State Scientific and Technical Library of Ukraine](/wiki/State_Scientific_and_Technical_Library_of_Ukraine "State Scientific and Technical Library of Ukraine"). According to the [SSTL](/wiki/State_Scientific_and_Technical_Library_of_Ukraine "State Scientific and Technical Library of Ukraine"), it could be used in future cooperation between the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), in which "Ukrainian will soon, along with other European languages, take its rightful place in multilingual natural language processing scenarios, including machine translation."[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The Derzhstandart 1995 system (invented by Maksym Vakulenko) is also mentioned in the DSTU 9112:2021 standard (approved in 2022) as the "B system"; the new standard also includes an "A system" with diacritical marks and some differences from ISO 9:1995: г=ğ, ґ=g, є=je, и=y, і=i, х=x, ь=j, ю=ju, я=ja.

#### ISO 9

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Ukrainian&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: ISO 9")]

[ISO 9](/wiki/ISO_9 "ISO 9") is a series of systems from the [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization"). The ISO published editions of its "international system" for romanization of Cyrillic as recommendations (ISO/R 9) in 1954 and 1968, and standards (ISO 9) in 1986 and 1995. This was originally derived from scientific transliteration in 1954 and is meant to be usable by readers of most European languages. The 1968 edition also included an alternative system identical to the British Standard.[[17]](#cite_note-:0-17)

The 1995 edition supports most national Cyrillic alphabets in a single transliteration table. It is a pure transliteration system, with each Cyrillic character represented by exactly one unique Latin character, making it reliably reversible, but sacrificing readability and adaptation to individual languages. It considers only [graphemes](/wiki/Grapheme "Grapheme") and disregards [phonemic](/wiki/Phonemic "Phonemic") differences. So, for example, г ([Ukrainian He or Russian Ge](/wiki/He_(Cyrillic) "He (Cyrillic)")) is always represented by the transliteration *g*; ґ ([Ukrainian letter Ge](/wiki/Ge_with_upturn "Ge with upturn")) is represented by *g̀*.

Representing all of the necessary diacritics on computers requires Unicode, and a few characters are rarely present in computer fonts, for example g-grave: g̀.

#### Ukrainian National transliteration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Ukrainian&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Ukrainian National transliteration")]

This is the official system of Ukraine, also employed by the United Nations and many countries' foreign services. It is currently widely used to represent Ukrainian geographic names, which were almost exclusively romanized from Russian before Ukraine's independence in 1991, and for personal names in passports. It is based on [English orthography](/wiki/English_orthography "English orthography"), and requires only [ASCII](/wiki/ASCII "ASCII") characters with no diacritics. It can be considered a variant of the "modified Library of Congress system", but does not simplify the -ий and -ій endings.

Its first version was codified in Decision No. 9 of the Ukrainian Committee on Issues of Legal Terminology on April 19, 1996,[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-:3-22) stating that the system is binding for the transliteration of Ukrainian names in English in legislative and official acts.

A new official system was introduced for transliteration of Ukrainian personal names in [Ukrainian passports](/wiki/Ukrainian_passport "Ukrainian passport") in 2007.

An updated 2010 version became the system used for transliterating all proper names and was approved as Resolution 55 of the [Cabinet of Ministers of Ukraine](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Ministers_of_Ukraine "Cabinet of Ministers of Ukraine"), January 27, 2010.[[23]](#cite_note-Decision_no._55-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) This modified earlier laws and brought together a unified system for official documents, publication of cartographic works, signs and indicators of inhabited localities, streets, stops, subway stations, etc.

It has been adopted internationally. The 27th session of the UN Group of Experts on Geographical Names ([UNGEGN](/wiki/UNGEGN "UNGEGN")) held in New York 30 July and 10 August 2012 after a report by the [State Agency of Land Resources of Ukraine](/w/index.php?title=State_Agency_of_Land_Resources_of_Ukraine&action=edit&redlink=1 "State Agency of Land Resources of Ukraine (page does not exist)") (now known as Derzhheokadastr: Ukraine State Service of Geodesy, Cartography and Cadastre) experts[[25]](#cite_note-25) approved the Ukrainian system of romanization.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The BGN/PCGN jointly adopted the system in 2019.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Official geographic names are romanized directly from the original Ukrainian and not translated. For example, *Kyivska oblast* not *[Kyiv Oblast](/wiki/Kyiv_Oblast "Kyiv Oblast")*, *Pivnichnokrymskyi kanal* not *[North Crimean Canal](/wiki/North_Crimean_Canal "North Crimean Canal")*.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

#### Romanization for other languages than English

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Ukrainian&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Romanization for other languages than English")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Peremy%C4%8Dka%2C_turistick%C3%A9_zna%C4%8Den%C3%AD.jpg/250px-Peremy%C4%8Dka%2C_turistick%C3%A9_zna%C4%8Den%C3%AD.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peremy%C4%8Dka,_turistick%C3%A9_zna%C4%8Den%C3%AD.jpg)

Czech transliteration of Ukrainian (Peremyčka, Jasiňa, U Stěpana) in Transcarpathia on the hiking fingerposts installed in 2010. However, the transliteration is not fully consistent – "Ust Corna" instead of "Usť Čorna", "Bliznica" instead of "Blyznycja" etc.

Romanization intended for readers of other languages than English is usually transcribed phonetically into the familiar orthography. For example, *y*, *kh*, *ch*, *sh*, *shch* for anglophones may be transcribed *j*, *ch*, *tsch*, *sch*, *schtsch* for German readers (for letters й, х, ч, ш, щ), or it may be rendered in Latin letters according to the normal orthography of another Slavic language, such as Polish or Croatian (such as the established system of scientific transliteration, described above).

Czech and Slovak standard transliteration uses letters with diacritics (ž, š, č, ď, ť, ň, ě) and letters i, y, j, h, ch, c in the local meaning. Diphthong letters are transcribed as two letters (ja, je, ji, ju, šč).[[29]](#cite_note-29) Czech transliteration was used, for example, on [hiking signs](/wiki/Czech_Hiking_Markers_System "Czech Hiking Markers System") in Transcarpathia, which was established according to the methodology of the [Czech Tourists Club](/wiki/Czech_Tourist_Club "Czech Tourist Club") – the Ukrainian markers replaced that later with the English transcription.[[30]](#cite_note-30) However, the fact that Ukraine itself has started to use English transliteration on its documents and boards, also influences the practice in Czech and Slovak, which is also penetrated by English transliteration of Ukrainian.

#### Ad hoc romanization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Ukrainian&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Ad hoc romanization")]

Users of public-access computers or mobile [text messaging services](/wiki/Short_message_service "Short message service") sometimes improvise informal romanization due to limitations in keyboard or character set. These may include both sound-alike and look-alike letter substitutions. Example: *YKPAIHCbKA ABTOPKA* for "УКРАЇНСЬКА АВТОРКА". See also Volapuk encoding.

This system uses the available character set.

#### Ukrainian telegraph code

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Ukrainian&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Ukrainian telegraph code")]

For telegraph transmission. Each separate Ukrainian letter had a 1:1 equivalence to a Latin letter. Latin Q, W, V, and X are equivalent to Ukrainian Я (or sometimes Щ), В, Ж, Ь. Other letters are transcribed phonetically. This equivalency is used in building the [KOI8-U](/wiki/KOI8-U "KOI8-U") table.

### Transcription

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Ukrainian&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Transcription")]

See also: [Ukrainian phonology](/wiki/Ukrainian_phonology "Ukrainian phonology") and [Ukrainian alphabet](/wiki/Ukrainian_alphabet "Ukrainian alphabet")

[Transcription](/wiki/Transcription_(linguistics) "Transcription (linguistics)") is the representation of the spoken word. [Phonological](/wiki/Phonological "Phonological"), or phonemic, transcription represents the [phonemes](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme"), or meaningful sounds of a language, and is useful to describe the general pronunciation of a word. [Phonetic](/wiki/Phonetic "Phonetic") transcription represents every single sound, or [phone](/wiki/Phone_(phonetics) "Phone (phonetics)"), and can be used to compare different dialects of a language. Both methods can use the same sets of symbols, but linguists usually denote phonemic transcriptions by enclosing them in slashes / ... /, while phonetic transcriptions are enclosed in square brackets [ ... ].

IPA

The [International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet") precisely represents pronunciation. It requires a special Unicode font.

## Conventional romanization of proper names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Ukrainian&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Conventional romanization of proper names")]

In many contexts, it is common to use a modified system of transliteration that strives to be read and pronounced naturally by [anglophones](/wiki/English_language "English language"). Such transcriptions are also used for the surnames of people of Ukrainian ancestry in English-speaking countries (personal names have often been translated to equivalent or similar English names, e.g., "Alexander" for *Oleksandr*, "Terry" for *Taras*).

Typically, such a modified transliteration is based on the [ALA-LC](/wiki/ALA-LC_romanization "ALA-LC romanization"), or Library of Congress (in North America), or, less commonly, the British Standard system. Such a simplified system usually omits [diacritics](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic") and ligatures (tie-bars) from, e.g., *i͡e*, *ï* or *ĭ*, often simplifies *-yĭ* and *-iĭ* word endings to "-y", omits romanizing the Ukrainian [soft sign](/wiki/Soft_sign "Soft sign") (*ь*) and apostrophe (*['](/wiki/Apostrophe "Apostrophe")*), and may substitute *ya, ye, yu, yo* for *ia, ie, iu, io* at the beginnings of words. It may also simplify doubled letters. Unlike in the [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language") where an apostrophe is punctuation, in the [Ukrainian language](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language") it is a letter. Therefore sometimes [Rus'](/wiki/Rus_(name) "Rus (name)") is translated with an apostrophe, even when the apostrophe is dropped for most other names and words.

Conventional transliterations can reflect the history of a person or place. Many well-known spellings are based on transcriptions into another Latin alphabet, such as the German or Polish. Others are transcribed from equivalent names in other languages, for example Ukrainian *Pavlo* ("Paul") may be called by the Russian equivalent *Pavel*, Ukrainian *[Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv")* by the Russian equivalent *Kiev*.

The employment of romanization systems can become complex. For example, the English translation of Kubijovyč's *Ukraine: A Concise Encyclopædia* uses a modified Library of Congress (ALA-LC) system as outlined above for Ukrainian and Russian names—with the exceptions for endings or doubled consonants applying variously to personal and geographic names. For technical reasons, maps in the Encyclopedia follow different conventions. Names of persons are anglicized in the encyclopedia's text but also presented in their original form in the index. Various geographic names are presented in their anglicized, Russian, or both Ukrainian and Polish forms, and appear in several forms in the index. Scientific transliteration is used in linguistics articles. The Encyclopedia's explanation of its transliteration and naming convention occupies 2-1/2 pages.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

2. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-word-i_50-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-word-i_50-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-word-i_50-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-word-i_50-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-word-i_50-4) [***f***](#cite_ref-word-i_50-5) [***g***](#cite_ref-word-i_50-6) [***h***](#cite_ref-word-i_50-7) [***i***](#cite_ref-word-i_50-8) [***j***](#cite_ref-word-i_50-9) [***k***](#cite_ref-word-i_50-10) [***l***](#cite_ref-word-i_50-11) The second transliteration is used word-initially.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-51)** National (1996) system: transliteration can be rendered in a simplified form. Doubled consonants ж, х, ц, ч, ш are simplified, for example Запоріжжя = *Zaporizhia*. Apostrophe and soft sign are omitted, but always render ьо = *'o* and ьї = *'i*.
4. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-word-yi_52-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-word-yi_52-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-word-yi_52-2) *Yi* is used at the beginning of words, *i* is used in other positions
5. **[^](#cite_ref-word-iva_53-0)** Word-initially, after vowels or after the apostrophe.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-after-c_54-0)** After consonants.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-apos_55-0)** Apostrophe is used before iotated *ja*, *ju*, *je*, *ji*, *jo*, and to distinguish the combination ьа (*j'a*) in compound words from я (*ja*), for example, Волиньавто = *Volynj'avto*.
